items.title: North Korean forces suffering 'mass casualties' on Russian frontlines with Ukraine | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy says several wounded North Korean soldiers captured by Ukraine while fighting in Russia have died. He accused Russia of throwing them into battle with quote "minimal protection". Pyongyang has sent an estimated 11,000 troops to support the Russian armed forces. 

North Koreans are reportedly being deployed in the Kursk region - the Russian territory captured by Ukraine over the summer. US officials say North Korean forces are suffering "mass casualties" there on the front lines, with around a thousand soldiers killed or wounded this past week alone.

Here's Volodymyr Zelenskyy speaking about the North Korean soldiers captured by Ukraine: ''The Russians send them into assaults with minimal protection. Today, there were reports about several soldiers from North Korea. Our soldiers managed to capture them, but they were very seriously wounded and could not be revived. This is one of the manifestations of the madness that dictatorships are capable of. The Korean people should not lose their people in battles in Europe. And this can be influenced by Korea's neighbors, particularly China. If China is sincere in its statements that the war should not expand, appropriate influence on Pyongyang is needed.''
_
Jenny Town is a Senior Fellow at the US think tank Stimson Center and the Director of Stimson’s 38 North Program which provides analysis of events in and around North Korea.

Edward Howell is a lecturer in International Relations at the University of Oxford and an expert on Korea.

00:00 Who are the the troops N. Korea sends to the Russia-Ukraine war?
00:55 Jenny Town, North Korea analyst | Stimson Center

items.title: Why Germany's far-right AfD is set to break records | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Elon Musk says there's only one political party that can save Germany. The Tesla billionaire and key advisor to Donald Trump, sent shockwaves around Europe when he backed the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) on a post on his X platform. The AfD is on track for its best result ever in Germany’s upcoming elections, tipped to take second place behind the conservatives. What are the drivers of this growth? How would it change Germany and its place in the world? And who are the key people shaping the party’s anti-immigration, Eurosceptic agenda? DW’s Richard Walker, Matthew Moore, and Stefanie Witte of the Tagesspiegel newspaper discuss in this Berlin Briefing recorded on December 13th.

CHAPTERS

items.title: South Korea's parliament votes to impeach acting president Han Duck-soo | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
South Korean lawmakers have voted to impeach their acting president Han Duck-Su. He's the second president to be impeached in just two weeks. The opposition accused Han of obstructing due process after his predecessor Yoon Suk Yeol was impeached before Christmas for briefly declaring martial law. Both former presidents will now face a ruling by the constitutional court which will decide if the impeachments can be upheld.

For more on this, we are joined by Fabian Kretschmer. He is a journalist who is based in Seoul but is currently in Berlin.

items.author: DW News
items.content:

Officials in the US and Azerbaijan, as well as some aviation experts, say the plane was likely targeted by a Russian surface-to-air missile which mistook the plane for a Ukrainian drone. The plane was traveling from Baku, Azerbaijan, to the Russian city of Grozny when it veered far off course and crashed. Azerbaijan has observed a day of mourning.

items.title: Media freedom groups urge protection of Palestinian journalists | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Palestinian health authorities say two separate Israeli strikes killed at least ten people in Gaza. The strikes hit Gaza City in the north and Nuseirat in central Gaza. Of the ten people killed, five were journalists from the Al-Quds Today television network, which often features leaders from the Palestinian Islamic Jihad militant group in its reporting. They were killed when their vehicle, clearly marked as press, was hit outside a hospital at the Nuseirat refugee camp. The Israeli military said it targeted militants "posing" as journalists. Media freedom watchdogs Reporters without Borders (RSF) and the Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ) condemned this recent Israeli attack as well as the increasing death toll of media workers in Gaza.

items.title: Airstrikes kill 10 in Gaza as Israel advances into Golan's demilitarized buffer zone | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Palestinian health authorities say at least ten people have been killed in separate Israeli strikes in Gaza. 

The strikes hit Gaza City in the north and Nuseirat in central Gaza. The toll includes five journalists from the Al-Quds televison network, which is affiliated with the Palestinian Islamic Jihad militant group. They were killed when their vehicle was hit outside a hospital. The Israeli military said it targeted the group's militants at the Nuseirat refugee camp. 

Israel's government has approved plans to double the population of Israeli settlers in the occupied Golan Heights, an area of Syria that Israel unilaterally annexed in 1981. The government's move comes in the wake of the fall of the Assad regime in Syria. The Israeli army has also advanced into Golan's demilitarized buffer zone in Syria and launched hundreds of airstrikes on Syrian military targets. DW Correspondent Emily Gordine reports from Ein Zivan in the occupied Golan Heights.

items.title: How disaster prepared are countries in case of a tsunami? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Countries in Southeast Asia are marking 20 years since a massive tsunami swept ashore, killing more than 220,000 people. In 2004 a powerful earthquake struck off Indonesia's western coast, unleashing the tsunami that hit more than a dozen countries. People in Indonesia's Aceh province have been holding memorial services to pay tribute to the victims. People there have also been offering prayers at mass graves to pay tribute to the victims. 

Countries in Southeast Asia are marking 20 years since a massive tsunami swept ashore, killing more than 220,000 people. The deadly waves were triggered by a 9.1 earthquake that struck off the coast of the Indonesian island of Sumatra on December 26, 2004. The tsunami rippled across the Indian Ocean killing thousands in Malaysia, Thailand, India, and Sri Lanka. Most of the victims were killed in Indonesia.  

Since then, the Indonesia has stepped up its disaster preparedness but some question whether it’s enough. Scientists say the next big quake is only a matter of time.

items.title: Might Trump approve an Israeli annexation of the West Bank, or force Netanyahu to curb settlements?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Illegal Israeli settlement activity has forcibly expelled many Palestinians from their homes. Far-right members of the Israeli government, including Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich and National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir, are calling for an illegal annexation of the occupied West Bank. Israel's governent is supporting the expansion of illegal Israeli settlements are expanding, and its far-right ministers are planning on taking over what's known as Area C in the West Bank. Whether President-elect Trump will impose limitations on this illegal activity or greenlight Israel's annexation of the occupied West Bank remains to be seen.

items.title: Passenger jet crash in Kazakhstan kills dozens, rescue efforts underway | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Rescue efforts are underway at the site of a plane crash near the city of Aktau in Kazakhstan. The transport ministry says a total of 67 passengers and crew were on board the Azerbaijan Airlines flight. The number of reported survivors continues to vary. 

A video verified by AP news agency appears to show the moment the plane crashed. It was en route from Azerbaijan's capital Baku to the Russian city of Grozny.

items.title: Can Ukraine protect its energy grid using aid in third winter of war? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Authorities in Ukraine are reporting a massive Russian aerial attack across the country. The northeastern city of Kharkiv is among the hardest hit, with at least six injured. The strikes are targeting the country's energy networks, which have led to regular power black-outs.  

Ukraine is now in its third winter of war. DW correspondent Max Zander reports from the capital Kyiv on how communities are coping with the hardship and disruption to daily life.

items.title: Syria post-Assad: New leader dissolves rebel factions | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syria's new administration says some of the rival rebel factions have agreed to disband and merge into a single force. 

The country's de-facto leader Ahmed al-Sharaa has reached an understanding with faction chiefs to dissolve their groups and unite under the defense ministry. The interim prime minister Mohammed al-Bashir says the ministry will be restructured to include former rebels and officers from the army of the ousted dictator Bashar al-Assad. One major group has held out from disarming so far - the Kurdish-led Syrian Defense Forces, and their People's Protection Units, also known as the YPG. 

Giorgio Cafiero further explains the deal. He is a Middle East Analyst and CEO and founder of Gulf State Analytics, a geopolitical risk consultancy based in Washington DC.

00:00 Syrian rebel groups agree to merge, but not all 
00:55 Giorgio Cafiero, Gulf State Analyst
